# Beyond avoided losses: capitalizing on the secondary benefits of investments in urban flood protection

![Water retention pond.](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/wbcollab/blog-water_2?qlt=90&fmt=webp&resMode=sharp2 "Water retention pond.")

Faced with increased climate variability, cities are increasingly considering investments in urban flood protection. However, when evaluating infrastructure investments, they often consider only the direct benefits derived from avoided losses with the costs of infrastructure works, neglecting the many secondary benefits that these investments can bring. Take Bangkok's [Centennial Park](https://edition.cnn.com/style/article/bangkok-centennial-park/index.html). It serves not only as a retention area for floodwaters but also as an urban park that integrates nature-based solutions to provide valuable space for recreation and protection from heat for citizens.

![Stages of table making](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/wbcollab/picture1?qlt=90&fmt=webp&resMode=sharp2)

By considering these and other secondary benefits in the decision-making processes, we can better understand the true value of flood protection and make more informed choices for our communities. It's time to start taking these benefits seriously.

### Types of Secondary Benefits of Flood Protection

A recent study by [Doeffinger and Rubinyi (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479722021909), with support from Global Facility for Disaster Reduction and Recovery’s (GFDRR) [City Resilience Program](https://www.gfdrr.org/en/crp), highlights how [an investment in urban flood protection can have many additional or secondary benefits across several areas: society, the economy, the environment, and infrastructure systems.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=an+investment+in+urban+flood+protection+can+have+many+additional+or+secondary+benefits+across+several+areas%3A+society%2C+the+economy%2C+the+environment%2C+and+infrastructure+systems.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/beyond-avoided-losses-capitalizing-secondary-benefits-investments-urban-flood?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank)

**Societal benefits:** Flood protection measures can provide a safe and resilient environment, reducing the physical and psychological impacts of flooding on communities. Parks and green spaces designed to manage floodwaters can also provide recreational opportunities and community gathering spaces.

**Economic benefits:** Flood protection measures can increase proximal property values, reduce business disruption, and attract new investment to the area. Redevelopment on formerly flood-prone waterfronts made possible by flood protection measures like embankments can provide new economic opportunities, including housing, commercial development, and tourism.

**Environmental benefits:** Flood protection measures can protect and enhance the natural environment by reducing erosion, improving water quality, and promoting biodiversity. Green infrastructure solutions, like wetlands, can manage stormwater while also providing habitats for wildlife and improving the aesthetic quality of urban areas.

**Greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction benefits:** Flood protection measures can play a role in reducing GHG emissions by incorporating low-carbon urban mobility options and green spaces within their design. By integrating greenways and bike paths alongside embankments, cities can promote sustainable transportation. Moreover, green spaces that have been integrated into the project design can function as a carbon sink.

### Why Aren’t Secondary Benefits Being Considered

[While the benefits of flood protection measures beyond risk reduction are well documented, rarely are they fully incorporated into economic analyses.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=While+the+benefits+of+flood+protection+measures+beyond+risk+reduction+are+well+documented%2C+rarely+are+they+fully+incorporated+into+economic+analyses.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/beyond-avoided-losses-capitalizing-secondary-benefits-investments-urban-flood?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank) This is largely due to the difficulty in quantifying them and the questions they raise around the necessary spatial and temporal scales. The study on secondary benefits of urban flood protection by Doeffinger and Rubinyi (2023) examined 20 urban flood protection projects financed by international finance institutions, finding that only seven of them mentioned secondary benefits and only three quantified and included them in the cost-benefit analysis.

One of the projects that did calculate multiple types of secondary benefits was the [Can Tho Urban Development and Resilience Project](https://documents.worldbank.org/en/publication/documents-reports/documentdetail/316951467987903833/vietnam-can-tho-urban-development-and-resilience-project). The project utilized the World Bank’s [triple dividend of resilience](https://documents.worldbank.org/en/publication/documents-reports/documentdetail/993161515193991394/the-triple-dividend-of-resilience-realizing-development-goals-through-the-multiple-benefits-of-disaster-risk-management) framework to assess project benefits and costs. Under a base case (i.e. assuming no alternative scenarios), potential secondary benefits from the project accounted for 12.7% of the project’s net present value of total benefits. The [Liberia Urban Resilience Project](https://projects.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/project-detail/P169718) also utilized the triple divide framework and assessed the potential increase in economic activity by using a fixed percentage of the direct losses. The assumption being that investors would be more likely to finance projects due to an increased sense of safety or reduced risk. Nevertheless, the limited consideration of secondary benefits in the majority of the 20 projects’ economic analyses assessed highlights the need for improved methods to capture and quantify these benefits, as they can play a significant role in the decision-making and approval process for flood protection infrastructure investments.

### Paths Forward

[Climate change is already affecting our cities, and there is an urgent need for adaptation, including investing in flood protection measures.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Climate+change+is+already+affecting+our+cities%2C+and+there+is+an+urgent+need+for+adaptation%2C+including+investing+in+flood+protection+measures.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/beyond-avoided-losses-capitalizing-secondary-benefits-investments-urban-flood?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank) An approach that fails to integrate the multiple benefits of flood protection risks resulting in sub-optimal levels of protection and missed opportunities for enhancing urban resilience. To fully realize the benefits of urban flood protection investments and better prepare for the challenges of climate change, it is crucial to consider the secondary benefits they can bring. Here are three action points to help incorporate these benefits into the decision-making process:

By taking these actions, we can make more informed decisions about flood protection investments and fully realize the potential benefits they can bring to our cities.

# The Economic Impact of Flooding: Why Investing in Flood Defence Matters

### FCI BLOGS

### FCI March Blog - The Economic Impact of Flooding: Why Investing in Flood Defence Matters

Flooding is one of the most devastating natural disasters, with far-reaching economic, social, and environmental consequences. As climate change accelerates, the frequency and severity of floods are increasing globally, posing significant challenges to communities, governments, and economies. In this blog we explore the economic impact of flooding and look at why investing in flood defence infrastructure is not only a necessity but also a sound economic decision.

**The Growing Threat of Flooding**Flooding has become a pressing issue in recent years, with extreme weather events becoming more frequent and intense. In 2024 alone, the United States experienced 27 billion-dollar extreme weather events, causing an estimated $182 billion in damages. These events disrupt lives, destroy infrastructure, and result in significant economic losses, including business interruptions, property damage, and increased insurance premiums.

In the UK, flooding has similarly caused substantial economic and social distress. The government has acknowledged that years of underinvestment in flood defences have left critical infrastructure in poor condition, exacerbating the impact of recent storms.

**The Economic Costs of Flooding**Flooding imposes both direct and indirect costs on economies. Direct costs include physical damage to infrastructure, homes, and businesses, while indirect costs encompass long-term consequences such as reduced property values, increased insurance premiums, and disruptions to economic activity. Key economic impacts:

Property Damage and Loss- Flooding causes extensive damage to residential and commercial properties. For example, climate-related risks are projected to reduce real estate values in the U.S. by $1.4 trillion over the next 30 years! This loss in value not only affects homeowners but also has broader implications for local economies and tax revenues.

Business Disruption- Flooding disrupts business operations, leading to lost productivity and revenue. The economic cost of businesses being unable to trade normally during floods is significant, particularly in regions heavily reliant on industries such as agriculture, manufacturing and tourism.

Insurance Costs- Rising flood risks have led to higher insurance premiums for property owners. In the U.S., insurance has become the fastest-growing expense for building owners, with some insurers exiting high-risk markets altogether. In flood-prone areas, premiums under the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) are expected to rise by up to 18% in 2025.

Mental Health and Social Costs- Beyond financial losses, flooding takes a toll on mental health and community well-being. The stress and trauma associated with displacement, property loss, and rebuilding efforts are often long-lasting.

**Investing in Flood Defence**Investing in flood defence infrastructure is a proactive approach to mitigating the economic and social impacts of flooding. Such investments offer significant benefits, including avoided costs, economic growth, and enhanced community resilience. Key reasons why flood defence investments are essential:

Cost-Benefit Analysis- Flood defence measures often provide high returns on investment. Research indicates that moderate adaptation measures can reduce flood damage by up to 90% in the EU and UK by 2100. In some cases, the benefit-to-cost ratio (BCR) of flood protection investments can reach as high as 120, meaning every dollar invested generates $120 in benefits.

Economic Growth and Job Creation- Flood defence projects stimulate local economies by creating jobs and supporting businesses. For instance, Cedar Rapids, Iowa, leveraged investments in flood protection to drive business creation and job growth, demonstrating the economic multiplier effect of such initiatives.

Reduced Insurance Costs- Investing in flood defences can help stabilise insurance markets by reducing the frequency and severity of flood-related claims. This, in turn, lowers premiums for property owners and makes insurance more accessible in high-risk areas.

Long-Term Resilience- The right flood defence investments can provide long term protection for communities, safeguarding lives and livelihoods for decades.

**Global Examples of Flood Defence Investments**Singapore has committed an additional $5 billion to its Coastal and Flood Protection Fund, bringing the total to $10 billion since its establishment in 2020. This fund is designed to enhance the city-state’s resilience against rising sea levels and extreme weather events.

The UK government has allocated £2.65 billion to build or maintain up to 1,000 flood defences, protecting more than 66,000 properties. This investment supports economic growth by safeguarding businesses, jobs, and farmland.

**Act now**Flooding poses a significant threat to economies and communities worldwide, but proactive investments in flood defence can mitigate these risks and deliver substantial economic benefits. From reducing property damage and insurance costs to fostering economic growth and resilience, the case for flood defence investments is clear. Governments, businesses, and communities must prioritise these initiatives to protect lives, livelihoods, and the environment in the face of a changing climate.

By taking decisive action now, we can build a more resilient future and ensure that the economic and social costs of flooding are minimized.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Proactive investments in flood defenses reduce economic losses, protect infrastructure, and enhance community resilience against increasingly severe flooding due to climate change.
Integrating flood resilience into urban planning leads to safer cities, optimizing land use while incorporating green infrastructure to mitigate flood risks and improve environmental quality.
Upgrading current infrastructure to withstand flooding can extend its lifespan, reduce maintenance costs, and ensure continued service delivery during extreme weather events.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of government and private sector
enriched_points.expanded_content: Collaboration between government and private sectors is essential for funding and implementing flood resilience projects, fostering innovations, and ensuring sustainable community development.
Investing in resilience yields significant long-term benefits, including reduced recovery costs, enhanced economic stability, and improved quality of life for residents in flood-prone areas.
